Name: European Roller
Habitat: Open bushy plains, dry grasslands, cultivated areas with scattered trees
Best_seen_at: Desert National Park (Rajasthan), Little Rann of Kutch (Gujarat) during autumn passage
How_to_identify: Distinctive bright turquoise-blue head, neck, and underparts with a contrasting brownish-cinnamon back. In flight, shows brilliant dark blue flight feathers. Larger than the Indian Roller; lacks the dark crown and has a more uniform blue tail without the bright bands of the Indian Roller.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Turquoise blue, cinnamon brown, dark blue
Diet: Large insects (beetles, grasshoppers), small reptiles, and amphibians; typically hunts from a perch

Name: Oriental Dollarbird
Habitat: Dense forests, forest edges, clearings with tall trees
Best_seen_at: Namdapha National Park (Arunachal Pradesh), Thattekad Bird Sanctuary (Kerala)
How_to_identify: Stocky build with a dark brownish-black head and dark greenish-blue body. Highly distinctive thick, bright orange-red bill and legs. In flight, shows prominent pale blue circular patches (the 'dollars') on the wings. Distinctly darker and shorter-legged than the Indian Roller.
Diet: Mainly insects, often caught in flight (hawking) from high perches

Name: Stork-billed Kingfisher
Habitat: Well-wooded areas near large rivers, lakes, and coastal waters
Best_seen_at: Kaziranga National Park (Assam), Sundarbans National Park (West Bengal)
How_to_identify: Massive bird with a very large, blood-red bill. Brownish cap, yellow collar and underparts, and bright blue wings and tail. Sexes are similar. Much larger than any other Indian kingfisher; the massive bill and brownish cap prevent confusion with the White-throated Kingfisher.
Diet: Fish, frogs, crabs, rodents, and even young birds; dives into water from a lookout

Name: Brown-winged Kingfisher
Habitat: Mangrove forests, coastal creeks, and tidal rivers
Best_seen_at: Sundarbans National Park (West Bengal), Bhitarkanika Wildlife Sanctuary (Odisha)
How_to_identify: Similar in structure to the Stork-billed Kingfisher but with dark brown wings and mantle instead of blue. Bright orange-buff head and underparts with a massive red bill. Rump is a brilliant pale blue, visible in flight. Specifically restricted to mangrove habitats, unlike the more widespread Stork-billed.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Dark brown, orange-buff, bright blue rump, red bill
Diet: Primarily crabs and fish found in mangrove and estuarine environments

Name: Ruddy Kingfisher
Habitat: Deeply shaded forest streams, mangroves, and swamp forests
Best_seen_at: Sundarbans National Park (West Bengal), South Andaman Island
How_to_identify: Uniformly cinnamon-rufous (ruddy) plumage with a slight violet gloss on the back. Has a bright red bill and legs. A distinctive pale blue patch on the lower back is visible in flight. Easily distinguished from the White-throated Kingfisher by its lack of white and overall reddish coloration.
Diet: Fish, crustaceans, and large insects; prefers heavily shaded areas near water

Name: White-throated Kingfisher
Habitat: Diverse habitats including farmland, gardens, forests, and wetlands; often far from water
Best_seen_at: Widespread across India; common in Keoladeo National Park (Rajasthan)
How_to_identify: Bright blue back and wings, chestnut-brown head and belly, and a large white patch on the throat and breast. Large red bill. In flight, shows prominent white wing patches. Frequently found away from water, which distinguishes its behavior from the Common Kingfisher.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Blue, chestnut-brown, white throat, red bill
Diet: Highly varied; includes large insects, lizards, mice, frogs, and fish

Name: Black-capped Kingfisher
Habitat: Coastal mangroves, estuaries, and large inland rivers
How_to_identify: Velvety black cap contrasting with a white collar and throat. Deep purple-blue upperparts and rusty-buff underparts. Red bill. Similar to White-throated Kingfisher but distinguished by the black cap (instead of chestnut) and white collar.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Purple-blue, black, white, rusty-buff, red bill
Diet: Primarily fish and crustaceans; also takes large insects

Name: Collared Kingfisher
Habitat: Mangroves, coastal gardens, and coconut groves
Best_seen_at: Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Bhitarkanika Wildlife Sanctuary (Odisha)
How_to_identify: Blue-green to turquoise upperparts with a clean white collar and underparts. Large black bill with a pale base to the lower mandible. Distinguished from other coastal kingfishers by the lack of any rufous/chestnut tones and the presence of the white 'collar'.
Diet: Crabs are a primary food source; also fish and lizards

Name: Blue-eared Kingfisher
Habitat: Shaded forest streams and pools in evergreen forests
Best_seen_at: Western Ghats (e.g., Bhagwan Mahavir Wildlife Sanctuary, Goa), Northeast India
How_to_identify: Deep royal blue upperparts and orange-rufous underparts. Specifically distinguished from the Common Kingfisher by its deep blue ear-coverts (which are rufous in the Common Kingfisher). It is also a darker, more saturated blue and is strictly forest-dependent.
Diet: Fish, aquatic insects, and small crustaceans

Name: Common Kingfisher
Habitat: Lakes, rivers, canals, and village ponds
Best_seen_at: Widespread throughout India; Keoladeo National Park (Rajasthan), Periyar National Park (Kerala)
How_to_identify: Small kingfisher with greenish-blue upperparts and bright orange-rufous underparts. Features a distinctive rufous patch over the ears (ear-coverts). Female has an orange base to the lower mandible, whereas the male's bill is all black. Smaller and less robust than the White-throated Kingfisher.
Diet: Mainly small fish and aquatic insects; hunts by diving from a low perch over water
